_README: MOCK DATA for the /training course player. Structure: company/sessions/tracks/roster + modules + lessons. Each lesson has a `blocks` array (heading | text | list | callout | prompt | video | context-compare | context-file | context-file-test | prompt-builder). trackId 'all' = shown to everyone; otherwise the lesson only shows for that track (used for role-tailored exercises). Interactive blocks call the Fieldwork Worker (lib/fw-live.ts); they fall back to canned output when NEXT_PUBLIC_FW_WORKER is unset. Facilitator-led exercises (icebreaker, word cloud, policy co-creation) live in /training/facilitator, not here. To go live on a Google Sheet: store sessions/tracks/roster/modules as tabs, and lesson blocks as a markdown column converted on read. See lib/training.ts.

company.name: Rafanelli & Nahas
company.tagline: AI Literacy Training · built with Fieldwork AI

sessions.title: Day 1 — Foundations
sessions.subtitle: Context, prompting, and responsible use — hands-on, in real time.

sessions.title: Session 2 — Applied Workflows & Governance
sessions.subtitle: Turn fluency into real R&N workflows you can trust and reuse.

blocks.text: Today is hands-on. You'll build the skills by doing them, right here in this portal, on real Rafanelli & Nahas work — not slides about AI, but AI itself.

Think of AI as a brand-new assistant you're onboarding. A great assistant needs three things from you: background so they understand your world, clear direction when you hand off a task, and guardrails for what they should never do. That's exactly what today covers — context, prompting, and responsible use.

blocks.title: Two rules for the day
items: Bring real work. Every exercise uses an actual R&N task. You'll leave with things you built.
You own the result. AI drafts; you decide. Nothing leaves the building without a human signing off.

blocks.title: What you'll walk out with
items: A company context file every future prompt can use.
A prompt-building habit that gets far better output.
A team AI usage policy you helped write.

blocks.text: The single biggest lever on the quality of what you get out of AI is the context you put in. Same question, wildly different answers — depending on what the AI knows about you.

Think about seeing a new doctor. You don't just walk in and get a prescription — you fill out the paperwork, they take your history, they read your chart. All of that is context, and it's what lets them help you specifically instead of generically. Every AI conversation starts with no chart. You provide it.
Context lives in a few places: right inside a prompt, and — more powerfully — in a saved file or project instructions the AI reuses every time. Build it once; stop re-explaining yourself.
Run the same ask twice — once cold, once with context — and watch what changes.

blocks.scenario: You're weighing whether to renew a major office tenant at slightly below market to protect occupancy in a soft leasing year.
blocks.barePrompt: Should I renew this tenant below market or hold out for market rate?
blocks.contextBlock: Context: I'm a partner at Rafanelli & Nahas, a privately held Boise CRE owner/operator (~1M sq ft, long-hold strategy, tenant-service is our north star). The tenant occupies 22,000 sq ft in a Class-A downtown building, has been with us 9 years, always pays on time, and is asking for a 10-year renewal at ~8% under current market. Downtown office is soft; re-tenanting this space would likely take 12–18 months. Act as a skeptical co-owner. I want the case for, the case against, the assumptions it hinges on, and what would make this a mistake.
blocks.system: You are assisting a commercial real estate owner/operator. Be concise, direct, and practical.

blocks.text: Same model, same question. The only thing that changed is what it knew about your situation.
Run the same ask twice — once cold, once with context — and watch what changes.

blocks.scenario: A long-time tenant is 15 days past due on their CAM reconciliation balance and you need to nudge them without straining the relationship.
blocks.barePrompt: Write an email to a tenant about a late payment.
blocks.contextBlock: Context: I'm a property manager at Rafanelli & Nahas, a Boise CRE owner/operator whose north star is tenant service. Our voice is warm but firm — never adversarial. The tenant is James at Suite 400, Boise Plaza, with us 6 years, generally reliable. The March CAM reconciliation balance of $2,140 is 15 days past due. I want a short email that preserves the relationship, states the specifics, and asks for a payment date this week.
blocks.system: You are assisting a commercial real estate property manager. Be concise and match a warm-but-firm professional tone.

blocks.text: Same model, same ask. The only thing that changed is what it knew about you and the tenant.
Run the same ask twice — once cold, once with context — and watch what changes.

blocks.scenario: A rooftop HVAC unit is short-cycling and you want a fast, safe first-pass diagnostic before calling a vendor.
blocks.barePrompt: How do I fix an air conditioner that keeps turning on and off?
blocks.contextBlock: Context: I'm a building engineer at Rafanelli & Nahas in Boise. The unit is a ~10-ton Carrier rooftop package unit serving the 3rd floor of Boise Plaza. It's short-cycling — running about 2 minutes, shutting off, restarting a few minutes later. Outdoor temp is ~95°F. I've already checked that the filter is clean and the thermostat batteries are good. I'm comfortable with basic checks but not refrigerant work. Give me the most likely causes ranked, the safe checks I can do myself, and the point where I should stop and call a licensed HVAC tech.
blocks.system: You are assisting a commercial building maintenance engineer. Be practical and safety-first.

blocks.text: Same model, same problem. The only thing that changed is what it knew about your unit and what you'd already tried.

Now let's build R&N's shared context file together — the thing every future prompt can start from. Fill in the parts you know best. Don't worry about spelling or polish; more is more, and AI doesn't forget. (Voice dictation works great here — just talk.)

blocks.intro: You'll be assigned a section or two to focus on. Add to any that you can.

sections.label: Who we are
sections.hint: Company, history, size, that we're a Boise CRE owner/operator.

sections.label: What we do
sections.hint: Property types, our buildings, the kind of work we do day to day.

sections.label: What makes us different
sections.hint: How we go to market, what we're known for. (Scott's tenant-service ethos goes here.)

sections.label: Our culture & voice
sections.hint: Values, how we treat tenants and each other, how we write.

sections.label: What AI should always know
sections.hint: Terminology, tone guardrails, things it should never assume.

blocks.text: Gabe is assembling everyone's answers into one company file live. You'll each leave with it loaded and ready to use.
Here's the payoff. Write one real prompt for your work, then run it two ways — without your context, and with it. Feel the difference.

blocks.guidance: Write a real prompt — a tenant email, a summary, a note, anything from your week. Then run it both ways.
blocks.starterPrompt: Draft a short, friendly note letting a tenant know we'll be doing planned maintenance in their building next week.
blocks.system: You are assisting a commercial real estate team. Be concise and practical.

blocks.text: The average person types about 4 words into Google and about 20 into an AI prompt. Best practice is far more than that. A prompt isn't a search box — it's your chance to generate real work, and the quality tracks how much you put in.
One idea, a few moves

items: Start with the end in mind — describe what a great result looks like before anything else.
Give your reasoning, not just the request — the why, what you've tried, and a persona to take (a skeptic, a seasoned broker, a careful lawyer).
Turn it into a thought partner — ask it to interview you before it drafts.
Don't settle — the first response is a starting point. Demand more, better, the way you want it.

blocks.text: You don't need a paragraph every time — this is meant to speed you up, not slow you down. But on real, thorny work, the extra minute spent on the prompt pays for itself many times over.
Pick a challenge, then build the prompt one piece at a time. Watch it assemble into one strong paragraph at the bottom — that finished prompt is the point. Then run it against the lazy version.

challenges.label: Pressure-test a decision
challenges.text: You're deciding whether to sign a 10-year renewal with an anchor tenant at ~8% below market to lock in occupancy this year.

challenges.label: Draft a partner update
challenges.text: You need to write the monthly ownership update: occupancy, leasing pipeline, and the one risk you're watching.

chunks.label: Describe the end state
chunks.instruction: What are you trying to accomplish? What does a great result look like?
chunks.placeholder: e.g. a tight one-page analysis I could hand a co-owner

chunks.label: Give your reasoning + a persona
chunks.instruction: Why does this matter, what do you already know, and who should the AI act as?
chunks.placeholder: e.g. Act as a skeptical co-owner; we hold long-term and protect tenant relationships

chunks.label: Ask it to interview you
chunks.instruction: Invite the AI to ask you clarifying questions before it drafts.
chunks.placeholder: e.g. Before you answer, ask me anything that would sharpen this

blocks.genericOutput: It depends on several factors. You should consider the current market rate, the tenant's payment history, the cost of finding a new tenant, and your overall occupancy goals. Weigh the pros and cons and consult your team before deciding.
blocks.system: You are assisting a commercial real estate owner/operator. Be concise, direct, and practical.

blocks.text: Pick a challenge, then build the prompt one piece at a time. Watch it assemble into one strong paragraph at the bottom — that finished prompt is the point. Then run it against the lazy version.

challenges.label: The late-CAM email
challenges.text: A long-time tenant is 15 days past due on their CAM reconciliation balance and you need to nudge them without straining the relationship.

challenges.label: Summarize an amendment
challenges.text: Turn a redlined lease amendment into a plain-English summary of what changed and why it matters.

chunks.label: Describe the end state
chunks.instruction: What are you trying to accomplish? What does a great result look like?
chunks.placeholder: e.g. a warm-but-firm email that gets a payment date this week

chunks.label: Give your reasoning + a persona
chunks.instruction: Why does this matter, what's the situation, and who should the AI act as?
chunks.placeholder: e.g. Act as a seasoned property manager; this tenant's been with us 6 years and tenant service is our north star

chunks.label: Ask it to interview you
chunks.instruction: Invite the AI to ask you clarifying questions before it drafts.
chunks.placeholder: e.g. Before you write it, ask me anything that would make it land better

blocks.genericPrompt: Write me an email to a tenant.
blocks.genericOutput:
Dear Tenant,

We are writing to inform you regarding your account. Please contact our office at your earliest convenience to discuss. Thank you for your attention to this matter.

Sincerely,
Management
blocks.system: You are assisting a commercial real estate property manager. Match a warm-but-firm professional tone.

blocks.text: Pick a challenge, then build the prompt one piece at a time. Watch it assemble into one strong paragraph at the bottom — that finished prompt is the point. Then run it against the lazy version.

challenges.label: Field note → work order
challenges.text: Turn your rough note about a broken rooftop HVAC unit into a clean, structured work order.

challenges.label: First-pass diagnostic
challenges.text: A rooftop unit at Boise Plaza is short-cycling; you want likely causes and safe checks before calling a vendor.

chunks.label: Describe the end state
chunks.instruction: What are you trying to accomplish? What does a great result look like?
chunks.placeholder: e.g. a clean work-order entry with priority and next steps

chunks.label: Give your reasoning + a persona
chunks.instruction: What's the situation, what have you tried, and who should the AI act as?
chunks.placeholder: e.g. Act as a senior building engineer; 10-ton Carrier RTU, filter's clean, I don't do refrigerant work

chunks.label: Ask it to interview you
chunks.instruction: Invite the AI to ask you clarifying questions before it answers.
chunks.placeholder: e.g. Before you answer, ask me anything you need about the unit

blocks.genericPrompt: How do I fix an AC unit?
blocks.genericOutput: There are many possible reasons an AC unit may not work. Check the power, the thermostat, the filter, and the circuit breaker. If it still doesn't work, you may need to call a professional HVAC technician to inspect it.
blocks.system: You are assisting a commercial building maintenance engineer. Be practical and safety-first.

lessons.title: Now push back — iterate on real work

blocks.text: The first answer is never the last word. The best AI users don't accept a mediocre draft — they steer it. Let's practice that on something real to you.

blocks.intro: Bring a real task from your week — a tenant email, a summary, a note, a plan. Get a first draft, then push back once and watch it get sharper.
blocks.system: You are assisting a commercial real estate team at Rafanelli & Nahas (a Boise owner/operator; tenant service is the north star). Be concise, practical, and match a warm, professional tone.

blocks.text: We've covered how to get great output. Just as important: how to use this safely. The confidence to use AI comes from knowing the boundaries.

blocks.title: The essentials
items: Your data stays yours — on our plan, what you enter isn't used to train models and doesn't leave our environment.
One sanctioned tool — we standardize on Claude. Never paste company or client info into random consumer tools.
Humans own the output — AI drafts; you decide. Your name, your judgment. "The AI did it" isn't a thing.
Verify what matters — dollar amounts, dates, clause language, legal terms. Trust it on low-stakes drafting.
Know the access boundary — AI can read and draft, but never sends or edits our system of record on its own.
Be transparent — if a client asks "do you use AI?", you should like your honest answer.

blocks.title: Next — together, out loud
blocks.text: We're going to write R&N's AI usage policy as a team, on screen. It's a shared agreement, not a rulebook handed down — which is exactly why we write it together. Gabe will capture it and we'll ratify it as a group in Session 2.

Everything you did today — giving context, prompting well, setting guardrails — is how you get the most out of Claude, the tool R&N chose. Before you leave, let's get it onto your own devices so it's ready Monday morning.

blocks.title: Get set up
items: Install Claude on your desktop and your phone, and sign in.
Load your company context file so every chat starts smart.
Try one real thing before Session 2 — bring what surprised you.

blocks.title: Where Claude fits
blocks.text: There are a handful of major AI providers (Anthropic — that's Claude — plus OpenAI, Google, and others). With Scott and Justin, R&N chose Claude for this work. Day 2 is where we go deep: refining AI's output, mapping your real workflows, and building reusable Skills.

lessons.title: Recap & your cheat sheet in action

blocks.text: Welcome back. Quick recap of Session 1, then we get to work building real R&N workflows.

items: Your company cheat sheet gives AI context — keep it handy and paste it at the start of serious work.
AI is strong on language and structure, weaker on facts you haven't given it — verify those.
The 4D loop: Delegate the right task, Describe it clearly, Discern what comes back, take Diligence for the result.

blocks.title: Warm-up (5 min)
blocks.text: Pull up your cheat sheet from Session 1 and read it with fresh eyes. Update anything that's changed or that you'd now add. We'll use it throughout today.
Before building anything, find the right thing to build. The best first workflow is repetitive, frequent, fairly standardized, and low-stakes if it's slightly off.

blocks.title: Exercise 1 — audit, categorize, prioritize (20 min)
items: Audit: list 5–10 tasks from your past week that felt repetitive or time-consuming. For each, note how often it happens, how long it takes, how standardized it is, and the cost if done imperfectly.
Categorize: sort them into AI can handle / AI assists, human decides / Human should handle.
Prioritize: pick the one task from the first two buckets that would save you the most time, and write one sentence on why it's your top pick.

blocks.text: Examples by role — Ops: drafting tenant notices, abstracting leases, prepping critical-date lists. Leadership: recurring partner/IC updates, first-pass market scans. Maintenance: work-order write-ups, vendor request emails, bid comparisons.
Take the task you prioritized and describe it precisely using the three parts of Description — Product, Process, Performance. This is the blueprint AI executes against.

items: Product: the end result, the inputs it receives, and the outputs it should produce.
Process: the step-by-step logic, the decision points, when to escalate to a human, and what documents it needs (FAQ, price list, cheat sheet).
Performance: the tone, how to handle uncertainty ("if unsure, flag — never guess"), and the hard nevers.

blocks.title: Exercise 2 — write it, then test it (25 min)
items: Write your Product / Process / Performance descriptions (2–3 sentences each).
Share them with AI along with 3–5 real R&N examples from your work.
Evaluate: did it categorize right? Accurate and on-tone? Did it fabricate anything?
Refine. Plan on 2–3 rounds before it feels reliable — that's normal.

blocks.title: Test-run scaffold
blocks.text:
[paste cheat sheet]
Here is an automation I want to build.
PRODUCT: [what you want back, inputs, outputs]
PROCESS: [steps, decision points, when to escalate]
PERFORMANCE: [tone, how to handle uncertainty, the nevers]

Here are 3–5 real examples to run it on:
[paste examples]

Run each example, then tell me where my description needs to be sharper.
Before you rely on a workflow, think through what could go wrong and how you'll catch it. Diligence is what makes automation safe to trust.

blocks.title: Exercise 3 — three kinds of diligence (20 min)
items: Creation: why is this task a good fit? List 3 failure modes (e.g. wrong pricing, templated reply to a complaint, mixing up tenants) and a safeguard for each.
Deployment: will you review every output or sample? When would you reduce oversight, and what evidence would you need? What would make you pause it entirely?
Transparency: who needs to know AI is involved, and how will you tell them?

blocks.title: Automation Readiness Statement
blocks.text: Wrap it up in 3–5 sentences: what you're automating, why it's appropriate, the safeguards in place, and how you'll be transparent. Keep it as a reference for you and your team.
A short, honest AI policy scales your judgment — so the whole team works from the same understanding instead of making it up case by case.

blocks.title: Exercise — write a one-page policy (20 min)
items: Reflect: What tasks are you now confident handing to AI? What will you always keep human? What do tenants and your team need to know?
Build it with AI: share your cheat sheet and those answers, and ask for a one-page policy with three sections — What we use AI for / What stays human / How we stay accountable.
Stress-test: if a tenant asked "do you use AI in your business?", does this give you a clear, honest answer? Fix anything you softened.

blocks.title: Draft the policy
blocks.text:
[paste cheat sheet]
Here's how I think about AI in our work:
- Confident to hand off: [list]
- Always human: [list]
- What people should know: [list]

Draft a one-page AI use policy for R&N with three sections: "What we use AI for" (approved tasks/tools), "What stays human" (and why), and "How we stay accountable" (oversight, transparency, what happens when something goes wrong). Plain language that sounds like us.

lessons.title: Staying the human in the loop

blocks.text: The point of all this isn't to remove people — it's to free you for the work only people can do.

blocks.title: Key takeaways
items: Being the human in the loop is more than oversight — you decide what problems AI should even solve.
Avoid dependency through understanding, not avoidance: if you can explain what the AI is doing, that's a good sign.
AI should free you for more human work, not less — the personal outreach, the relationship, the judgment call.
Decide as a team what happens with the time AI saves. Set that norm early.
Your AI policy is how this understanding scales as R&N grows.

lessons.title: Your task this week & what's next

blocks.text: Fluency comes from reps. Before you leave, commit to one real task you'll tackle with AI this week — your practice ground.

blocks.title: Final assignment
items: Pick one concrete task waiting for you (a draft, a market scan, a data summary, an onboarding doc, a plan).
Run the full 4D loop on it: Delegation → Description → Discernment → Diligence.
Afterward, jot down what worked, what needed correction, and which competency needs more practice.

blocks.text: Keep going

items: Keep a running doc of the prompts and workflows that work for you — so you're not starting from scratch, and teammates can pick them up.
Blueprints: step-by-step guides for common tasks at claude.com/resources/use-cases.
Stay current: rules around AI in business are evolving (FTC guidance, state privacy laws). Revisit these principles as things change.
Questions between sessions? gabe@getfieldworkai.com — that's what office hours are for.

blocks.title: You're done.
blocks.text: You've built a cheat sheet, learned the 4D Framework, mapped your opportunities, built and stress-tested a workflow, and written your AI policy. That's AI fluency. Now go get the reps.
